title: Basic operators
_authoring: Teaching arc (owner-directed, 2026-06-01): the intro teaches the three OPERATORS FIRST (AND → OR → NOT), each in three forms — its symbol, its meaning in code, and its logic-gate shape — and ONLY AFTER the operators does it teach what a TRUTH TABLE is (the grid, inputs-left/output-right, each row a situation, read-across, 2 inputs → 4 rows, the row order), then a worked example tracing A & B on that grid, then practice. Rationale: a learner cannot be shown a truth table and asked to fill it in before being told what it is; and the operators are the concepts, the table is the notation that records them. The deterministic intro stage machine (introAdvance.ts) walks EVERY explanation card in order before the worked example. Operator cards render their gate diagram + code form (IntroExplanation, keyed on `gate`); the truth-table card carries an illustrative AND grid. All truthTables verified MSB-first. Items: AND → OR → NOT → review-mix (A AND NOT B interleaves AND+NOT without nested composition); l1-and uses B AND A so the first practice forces retrieval, not a copy of the worked example's A & B.

lessonIntro.title: Lesson 1 — Logic: AND, OR, NOT
lessonIntro.body: In logic, every value is one of just two things: TRUE (we write 1) or FALSE (we write 0). Nothing in between. Logic is the set of rules for combining true/false values into new ones — and almost everything in computing is built from three operators that do exactly that: AND, OR, and NOT. We'll meet all three, then learn the tool that maps out what any operator does.

explanations.body:
AND combines two values and is true ONLY when BOTH of them are true. If either one is false, AND is false.

Think: "it is raining AND it is cold" — true only if both are true.

Symbol:  A & B
In code:  a && b   (true only when a and b are both true)
As a gate: the D-shaped AND gate below — two wires in, one out; the output is on only when both inputs are on.

OR combines two values and is true when AT LEAST ONE of them is true — including when both are. The only way OR is false is when both inputs are false.

Watch out: this is the INCLUSIVE or of logic. Everyday English 'or' often means 'one or the other but not both' — logic's OR says 1 OR 1 is still 1.

Symbol:  A || B
In code:  a || b   (true if a is true, or b is true, or both)
As a gate: the curved OR gate below — the output is on when either input is on.

NOT takes a SINGLE value and flips it: true becomes false, false becomes true. !1 = 0 and !0 = 1. It is the one operator here that takes just one input.

Think: "it is NOT raining" — true exactly when 'it is raining' is false.

Symbol:  !A
In code:  !a       (true when a is false)
As a gate: the triangle-with-a-bubble below — the little bubble is what means 'invert'. You'll see that same bubble reused on other gates later.

Now that you know the three operators, how do we write down EXACTLY what one does? With a truth table — a small grid that lists every possible input and the result for each.

How to read the grid below (it's AND):
• The left columns (A, B) are the INPUTS. The right column (Output) is what the operator gives back.
• Each ROW is one possible situation. Read a row across: "if A is this and B is this, the output is that."
• With 2 inputs there are exactly 4 situations, so 4 rows — A,B = 0,0 then 0,1 then 1,0 then 1,1.

Look at the AND table: only the bottom row (A=1, B=1) has output 1, because AND is true only when both inputs are true. Every other row has a 0 somewhere, so the output is 0. Reading the Output column top to bottom — 0, 0, 0, 1 — IS the definition of AND.

steps.label: List every situation as a row
steps.detail: Two inputs (A, B), each 0 or 1, gives 4 rows: 0,0 / 0,1 / 1,0 / 1,1.

steps.label: Go row by row and ask: is AND true here?
steps.detail: Row A=0,B=0 → not both 1 → output 0. Row 0,1 → not both 1 → 0. Row 1,0 → not both 1 → 0. Row 1,1 → both 1 → output 1.

steps.label: The Output column is the answer
steps.detail: Reading the output top to bottom: 0, 0, 0, 1. Your turn next.
